messages.content: You are a helpful, concise assistant for everyday how-to and troubleshooting.
Can you design a simple time‑blocked weekday plan for a grad student who works 20 hours a week, has classes on Monday and Wednesday, and wants evenings mostly free?

Here’s a clean template that keeps deep work in the mornings, classes mid‑day, and work in early afternoons so evenings are open. Adjust times to match your actual class slots.

Daily anchors:
- Morning routine: 7:30–8:00
- Deep work (no notifications): 8:00–10:30
- Lunch/reset: 12:00–12:30
- Admin/light tasks: 12:30–13:00
- Work block(s): early afternoon
- Exercise/errands: late afternoon as buffer
- Evenings: open for rest or optional light review

Sample week:
- Monday
  - 8:00–10:30 Deep study (problem sets/reading)
  - 11:00–12:30 Class
  - 13:00–15:00 Reading/review from class
  - 15:00–17:00 Work
  - 17:00–evening Free

- Tuesday
  - 8:00–11:00 Deep project block (thesis/lab)
  - 11:00–12:00 Break/errands
  - 13:00–17:00 Work
  - 17:00–evening Free

- Wednesday
  - 8:00–10:30 Deep study
  - 11:00–12:30 Class
  - 13:00–15:00 Office hours/group work
  - 15:00–17:00 Work
  - 17:00–evening Free

- Thursday
  - 8:00–11:00 Deep project block
  - 11:00–12:00 Break/meal prep
  - 13:00–17:00 Work
  - 17:00–evening Free

- Friday
  - 8:00–10:30 Weekly review and planning
  - 10:30–12:00 Catch‑up/overflow
  - 13:00–15:00 Work (or lab if needed)
  - 15:00–16:00 Admin/Email zero
  - 16:00–evening Free

Notes:
- This hits roughly 20 work hours across Tue/Thu 4 hours each plus Mon/Wed 2 hours each and Fri 2 hours. Adjust block lengths to meet your exact requirement.
- Protect the morning deep‑work blocks by batching email/messages for the afternoon.
- Keep a one‑hour flex buffer on at least two days for spillover tasks.
- Do a quick weekly plan Friday morning to roll priorities into next week.
